body:
Stephen LaBerge, the psychophysiologist who pioneered the scientific study of lucid dreaming, has spent a lifetime charting the fluid territories of consciousness. His work, which hands the power of awareness back to the dreamer, forces a question most of the spiritual world gets disastrously wrong: What is the difference between a dream and a vision? The distinction is not some subtle academic point; it is a chasm. Mistaking one for the other is a critical, and common, error on the path to liberation, leading countless souls not toward freedom, but deeper into the labyrinth of their own self-deception.

The Psyche’s Digestive Tract: What a Dream Really Is

For most of us, most nights, a dream is simply the psyche’s digestive process. It’s the chaotic, deeply personal soup made from the leftovers of your day: that tense conversation with your boss, a flicker of a childhood memory, the deep, ancient stories that churn in the collective human spirit. Dreams are where your mind tries to weave a narrative from the nonsensical, connecting the dots between the mundane and the mythic. The symbolism is messy, contradictory, and belongs entirely to you. The serpent in your dream isn’t from a universal dream dictionary; it is *your* serpent, colored by your specific fears, your hidden desires, and your unique relationship with that primal energy.

Think of your psyche as a vast, ancient library. Every experience, every thought, every emotion is a book on its shelves. At night, the librarian—your dreaming mind—wanders the stacks, pulling down volumes at random. A book about your financial anxieties falls open next to a dusty tome of medieval alchemy. A childhood photo album is juxtaposed with a futuristic sci-fi thriller. The dream is the story the librarian tells by stitching together these disparate elements. It is a collage, a remix, a wild, associative poem. It is not a prophecy. It is a reflection. It is a mirror showing you the contents of your own library, often in a distorted and funhouse-mirror way. To mistake this reflection for a literal truth is to get lost in the hall of mirrors, chasing phantoms of your own creation.

This digestive process is crucial. It’s how we metabolize our lives. But to grant every dream the status of a divine communiqué is a profound misunderstanding of the process. It’s like mistaking the gurgling of your stomach for the voice of God. The dream state, in its default mode, is a realm of symbolic processing, not divine proclamation. It is a space of emotional release, of psychological sorting, of the psyche trying to heal itself through narrative. To interrupt this process with grandiose interpretations is to interfere with the natural healing intelligence of your own being.

A resource I recommend on this topic is Dreams by C.G. Jung (paid link). Jung's collected writings on dreams from the Princeton Collected Works.

To treat every dream as a direct telegram from the divine is to get hopelessly lost. It’s giving the same weight to a stress dream about showing up to an exam naked as you would to a soul-shattering encounter with God. This isn’t to diminish dreams. They are a vital, indispensable source of information about your inner world. But they are the raw data, not the final report. They are the unedited dailies, not the finished film. Working with dreams means being a detective, a poet, and a psychotherapist all at once. It is the gritty work of sifting through the intuitive noise to find the signal.

A Direct Transmission: The Nature of Vision

A vision is a different beast entirely. It is not a digestive process; it is a direct transmission. A vision arrives with a startling clarity, a coherence, and a numinous quality that sets it apart from the background chatter of ordinary dreams. It feels less like something you created and more like something that was *given* to you. The symbolism in a vision often transcends the purely personal, touching on the archetypal and universal. The message is direct, potent, even if its full meaning takes time to unpack. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen that the failure to distinguish between a dream and a vision is one of the most common and damaging spiritual errors.

If you haven't screamed into a towel, dry-heaved your oppressive lineage into the Earth, or told a spiritual mentor to fuck off while holding full love in your heart — you might still be in spiritual kindergarten.

A vision does not feel like a collage. It feels like a masterpiece, painted by a hand far greater than your own. The colors are more vivid, the sounds are more resonant, the emotions are more profound. A vision has a weight, a gravity, that is unmistakable. It lands in your consciousness with the force of a thunderclap, and it leaves you shaken, changed, and irrevocably altered. It is not a reflection of your inner world; it is a revelation of a world beyond your own. It is a glimpse into the operating system of the universe. It is a direct download from the divine mainframe.

Unlike the personal, associative nature of a dream, a vision often speaks in the language of universal archetypes. The symbols are not just *your* symbols; they are the symbols of humanity. The message is not just for you; it is for the collective. A vision is a call to action, a mandate for change, a revelation of a truth that you are now responsible for carrying. It is not a puzzle to be solved; it is a fire to be tended. It is not a story about your past; it is a map for your future.

Visions carry a distinct energetic signature. They are often accompanied by a sense of awe, reverence, or the chilling feeling of being in the presence of something utterly holy. They can be terrifying, blissful, or both at once. But they are never, ever mundane. A vision is a punctuation mark in the long sentence of your life. It is a course correction from the cosmos. To treat a vision as just another dream is to receive a registered letter from the universe and toss it in the recycling bin.

May you shed the constricting veils that imprison you, arrive at a profound clarity, and enjoy an unbridled authenticity.

The Seduction of the Spiritual Bypass

The modern spiritual marketplace is a minefield of bypassing. It offers a thousand ways to avoid the brutal, messy work of self-inquiry in favor of a more palatable, shareable, and ultimately hollow version of enlightenment. This is dangerously true in dreamwork. It is far more comforting to believe your recurring dream of being chased is a romantic past-life memory than it is to confront the ways you are relentlessly running from your own truth in *this* life. It’s easier to label a confusing dream a “vision” from your “guides” than to do the agonizing work of untangling the knots of your own psyche.

You weren't wrong. You were just open and naive — in a system built on bullshit.

Spiritual bypassing is when you use your spiritual practice or beliefs to avoid addressing issues that are uncomfortable to you.

This isn’t a debate about the existence of past lives or spirit guides. The point is that jumping to those conclusions without a rigorous, mercilessly honest self-inventory is a form of spiritual cowardice. It’s using a spiritual concept to avoid a psychological reality. The real work of dream interpretation is not to collect a scrapbook of fantastic tales. It is to use the dream as a crowbar to pry open the door to your un-lived life, your un-felt emotions, and your un-spoken truths.

The Fierce Discipline of Discernment

So how do you tell the difference? How do you learn to separate the intuitive static of a dream from the clear, bell-like tone of a vision? It requires a fierce and unwavering commitment to self-honesty. It demands a willingness to be wrong, to be humbled, to admit that you don’t know. It requires you to sit in the fire of uncertainty without scrambling for the easy answer. You must develop a felt sense in your own body, to learn the energetic signature of truth, to know in your bones the difference between a comforting fantasy and a liberating reality.

This is not a mental exercise. It is a somatic one. It is the practice of dropping out of the story and into the body. When you recall a dream, where do you feel it in your body? Is there a tightness in your chest, a clenching in your gut, a buzzing in your hands? These are not random sensations. They are the language of your body, speaking the truth of the dream. A vision, on the other hand, often lands in the body with a sense of expansion, of alignment, of a deep and resonant rightness. It can be overwhelming, but it is rarely confusing. It is a full-body yes, even if the mind struggles to catch up.

One of the most potent tools for this is tracking your dreams over time. Keep a journal, but don’t just record the plot. Record your feelings, your associations, your bodily sensations upon waking. Notice the patterns that emerge over weeks and months. This is the raw material of your personal mythology. Learning to read it is like learning the language of your own soul. It is learning to speak the language of truth.

The past does not exist. It is a cancelled check, a forgotten wind, a story I created to feed my ego.

Frequently Asked Questions About Dreams and Visions

What if I can't remember my dreams?

An inability to remember dreams often signals a disconnect between the conscious and unconscious minds, a form of self-protection from what you’re not ready to face. To begin building a bridge, set a clear intention before sleep to remember. The moment you wake, before moving, grasp for any feeling or image and write it down, no matter how nonsensical; this simple act begins to strengthen the connection. It is a way of signaling to your psyche that you are ready to listen. It is a gesture of respect, of reverence, for the wisdom that lies dormant within you. And with time, with patience, with a consistent practice of listening, the dreams will begin to return. They will begin to speak to you again.

Normal is a setting on a washing machine. You, sweetheart, are not a washing machine.

Can a dream be both a dream and a vision?

The line isn’t always sharp. A vision can be embedded within a chaotic dream, a moment of profound clarity breaking through the noise. The work of discernment is to recognize these jewels without getting lost in the mud that surrounds them. This is not about neat categorization but about recognizing the signal when it cuts through the static, regardless of the surrounding dreamscape. It is the ability to be in the midst of a intuitive storm and still recognize the lighthouse. It is the practice of holding the tension of the opposites, of being able to say, “This part is just noise, but this part… this part is holy.”

What if my vision is terrifying?

Not all visions are of angels and light; some are of demons and darkness, showing us the parts of ourselves we have disowned. To run from these visions is to run from the most critical work of your life. The only way out is through. Facing the darkness is how you reclaim the power you’ve projected onto it, integrate your shadow, and become whole. A terrifying vision is not a curse. It is a gift. It is an invitation to wholeness. It is a map to the hidden treasure of your own soul. To follow that map, to face that terror, is to claim your birthright as a sovereign being.

How do I know if I'm just making it all up?

You are making it all up. And you are not. This is the central paradox. The psyche speaks in symbol and metaphor, not literal history. The question isn’t “Is it real?” but “What is it trying to show me?” Letting go of the need for factual certainty allows the vision to do its transformative work on you, with the proof of its truth found not in its facticity, but in its fruit. Does it make you more whole? More compassionate? More courageous? Does it lead you to a deeper connection with yourself, with others, with the divine? If the answer is yes, then it is real enough. It is true enough. It is holy enough.

The Real Work of The Difference Between a Dream

The work of distinguishing a dream from a vision is not an intellectual parlor game. It is a matter of spiritual life and death. It is the difference between wandering lost in the labyrinth of your own projections and finding the thread that leads to freedom. To do this work is to take a stand for your own soul, to refuse to settle for a cheap imitation of truth. It is to demand a direct, unmediated experience of the divine and be willing to do whatever it takes to get there.

This is the work of the spiritual adult. It is the willingness to take 100% responsibility for your own consciousness, to own your projections, to face your shadows, and to do the hard, unglamorous work of integration. It is the refusal to be a spiritual child, waiting for a guru, a guide, or a vision to save you. It is the recognition that you are the one you have been waiting for. You are the guru. You are the guide. You are the vision. This is not a metaphor. It is a literal truth. The divine is not some external force that you must appease or petition. It is the very ground of your being. To realize this is to come home.

Every time you set a real boundary — sovereign karma. Every time you say 'fuck off' when necessary — self-protective karma.

This work demands you become a warrior—a warrior of the heart and of the truth. It requires you to fight for your own liberation, to cut through the internal and external bullshit that keeps you small. It demands a willingness to feel everything, face everything, and integrate everything. There are no shortcuts, only the work. The work is hard, but its fruit is freedom. And freedom is everything. This is the fierce love that the world is starving for. A love that is not afraid of the dark. A love that is not afraid to burn.

Head to the forest, pound the Earth, and cry or scream with abandon! You are a work in progress, and growth is a messy business.

The path of the visionary is not for the faint of heart. It is for those willing to be undone, to be broken open, to be remade in the fire of their own truth. It is for those who are no longer willing to live a half-life of quiet desperation and spiritual bypassing. It is for those ready to claim their full inheritance as a child of the divine. Are you one of them? Are you willing to pay the price of admission to the visionary realm? Are you willing to let it all burn? The fire is waiting. And it is hungry.
